ppt.title: Life Processes - Session 1
ppt.presentationTitle: Understanding Life Processes: Nutrition and Respiration
ppt.presentationGoal: To enable students to define life processes, distinguish between autotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition, and explain the basic mechanism of respiration.

slides.teachingBeat: Set the stage for the lesson and engage students with a visual hook.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Life Processes: The Basics of Living
> Life Processes
> Nutrition & Respiration
- Grade 7-9 Science Session 1
- What makes something alive?
- The continuous processes of life
![Life Processes: The Basics of Living](http://host.docker.internal:3002/api/ppt-assets/020be5630ef74256f46ee6d10ada8b43fd5672ee.svg)

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: title-slide -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Set the stage for the lesson and engage students with a visual hook.
Purpose: Introduce the chapter topic.
Explain: Ask students what they think keeps them alive. Guide discussion to 'eating', 'breathing'. Define these as Life Processes.
Ask: "If a plant doesn't move, how do we know it's alive?"
Listen for: Responses about growth or photosynthesis.
Transition: Let's look at the specific processes that keep us going.
Explain: Today: Nutrition and Respiration
-->

slides.presentonMarkdown:
# What We Will Learn Today
> 1. Define Life Processes
- 2. Types of Nutrition: Auto & Hetero
- 3. The Role of Respiration
- Define 'Life Processes' clearly
- Distinguish Autotrophic vs Heterotrophic nutrition

<!-- layout: text-light -->
<!-- purpose: learning-outcomes -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Clarify expectations and provide a roadmap for the lesson.
Purpose: Set learning goals.
Explain: Read through the outcomes. Emphasize that 'Life Processes' are not just for humans but all living organisms.
Ask: "Which outcome do you think is most important?"
Listen for: Student opinions on nutrition vs respiration.
Transition: Let's start with the definition.
Explain: Explain why respiration is vital for energy
-->

slides.teachingBeat: Provide a clear, concise definition and examples.
mustTeach: Essential functions performed by living organisms
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# What Are Life Processes?
> Life Processes = Essential Functions
- - Maintain Body
- - Ensure Survival
- Key Examples:
- Nutrition | Respiration | Transport | Excretion

<!-- layout: text-light -->
<!-- purpose: core-concept-definition -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Provide a clear, concise definition and examples.
Purpose: Define the core concept.
Explain: Use an analogy. Just as a car needs fuel and maintenance to run, living things need these processes. Without them, life stops.
Ask: "Can you name any other process we do every day?"
Listen for: Breathing, eating, sleeping (clarify sleep is not a metabolic process in this context).
Board work: Write 'Nutrition', 'Respiration' on board as the two main topics today.
Transition: Let's dive into how organisms get their food.
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Provide a clear, concise definition and examples.
slides.studentTakeaway: Students understand that life processes are essential activities like nutrition, respiration, transport, etc., required for survival.
speakerNotes: Purpose: Provide a clear, concise definition and examples.
Purpose: Define the core concept.
Explain: Use an analogy. Just as a car needs fuel and maintenance to run, living things need these processes. Without them, life stops.
Ask: "Can you name any other process we do every day?"
Listen for: Breathing, eating, sleeping (clarify sleep is not a metabolic process in this context).
Board work: Write 'Nutrition', 'Respiration' on board as the two main topics today.
Transition: Let's dive into how organisms get their food.
slides.visualPlan: A labelled classroom diagram for What Are Life Processes? that makes the process or structure visible while the teacher explains core-concept-definition.

slides.teachingBeat: Differentiate between the two main modes of nutrition using a clear comparison.
mustTeach: Autotrophic: Make own food from inorganic sources
Heterotrophic: Depend on other organisms for food
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# How Do Organisms Get Food?
> Autotrophs (Self-feeders)
> - Use sunlight/chemical energy
- - Examples: Plants, Algae
- Heterotrophs (Other-feeders)
- - Depend on plants/animals
![How Do Organisms Get Food?](http://host.docker.internal:3002/api/ppt-assets/e3d6a963658d2f0efd2f9dc324ec595c99aa44aa.svg)

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: core-concept-nutrition-types -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Differentiate between the two main modes of nutrition using a clear comparison.
Purpose: Compare nutrition types.
Explain: Break down the Greek roots. 'Auto' means self, 'Hetero' means other. Autotrophs are producers; Heterotrophs are consumers.
Ask: "Are we autotrophs or heterotrophs? Why?"
Listen for: We eat food because we can't make it from sunlight.
Correct: Clarify that some bacteria are chemosynthetic (autotrophic) but don't use light, just chemicals. Focus on plants for now.
Transition: Let's look closer at how autotrophs do this.
-->
slides.templateSlideTitle: Types of Nutrition: Autotrophs vs Heterotrophs
slides.teacherIntent: Differentiate between the two main modes of nutrition using a clear comparison.
slides.studentTakeaway: Students can identify and explain the difference between making their own food (autotrophs) and consuming others (heterotrophs).
speakerNotes: Purpose: Differentiate between the two main modes of nutrition using a clear comparison.
Purpose: Compare nutrition types.
Explain: Break down the Greek roots. 'Auto' means self, 'Hetero' means other. Autotrophs are producers; Heterotrophs are consumers.
Ask: "Are we autotrophs or heterotrophs? Why?"
Listen for: We eat food because we can't make it from sunlight.
Correct: Clarify that some bacteria are chemosynthetic (autotrophic) but don't use light, just chemicals. Focus on plants for now.
Transition: Let's look closer at how autotrophs do this.
slides.visualPlan: Side-by-side comparison illustration. Left side: A green plant with sun rays and soil arrows pointing to it (labeled 'Inorganic'). Right side: An animal eating a leaf or fruit (labeled 'Organic Food').

assets.altText: Comparison diagram showing a plant making food and an animal consuming it.

animationHints: Highlight 'Autotroph' text on left, 'Heterotroph' on right

slides.teachingBeat: Explain the biological purpose of respiration and its basic chemical nature.
mustTeach: Respiration = Breaking down glucose for energy
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Why Do We Breathe? Respiration Explained
> Respiration Process:
> - Glucose + Oxygen -> Energy + CO2 + Water
- - Purpose: Power cellular activities
- - Happens in mitochondria
- Respiration = Breaking down glucose for energy
![Why Do We Breathe? Respiration Explained](http://host.docker.internal:3002/api/ppt-assets/15fa342948220e52b305ec4e04d54e91e2fa40c7.svg)

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: worked-example-respiration-basics -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Explain the biological purpose of respiration and its basic chemical nature.
Purpose: Explain respiration mechanism.
Explain: Distinguish between 'breathing' (physical act) and 'respiration' (chemical process). Use the analogy of burning fuel to release heat/energy. The body burns food for energy.
Ask: "Why do we breathe faster after running?"
Listen for: We need more oxygen to make more energy quickly.
Correct: Ensure students don't confuse breathing with respiration entirely; emphasize the cellular level.
Board work: Write 'Glucose + O2 -> Energy' on board.
-->
slides.slideTitle: Why Do We Breathe? Respiration Explained
slides.teacherIntent: Explain the biological purpose of respiration and its basic chemical nature.
slides.studentTakeaway: Students understand that respiration is about releasing energy from food, not just breathing air in and out.
bulletPoints: Respiration = Breaking down glucose for energy
speakerNotes: Purpose: Explain the biological purpose of respiration and its basic chemical nature.
Purpose: Explain respiration mechanism.
Explain: Distinguish between 'breathing' (physical act) and 'respiration' (chemical process). Use the analogy of burning fuel to release heat/energy. The body burns food for energy.
Ask: "Why do we breathe faster after running?"
Listen for: We need more oxygen to make more energy quickly.
Correct: Ensure students don't confuse breathing with respiration entirely; emphasize the cellular level.
Board work: Write 'Glucose + O2 -> Energy' on board.
slides.visualPlan: Simple schematic diagram showing a cell with mitochondria highlighted. Arrows show glucose and oxygen entering, energy (lightning bolt icon) being released, and CO2/water exiting.

assets.searchQuery: cellular respiration diagram simplified cell mitochondria glucose oxygen input energy output carbon dioxide water educational illustration clean lines blue green palette
assets.altText: Schematic of a cell showing respiration inputs and outputs.

animationHints: Animate arrows flowing into the mitochondria

slides.teachingBeat: Assess understanding and reinforce key takeaways.
mustTeach: 3. Name one life process other than nutrition.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Did We Get It? Quick Review
> Quick Review:
- - Autotroph = Makes own food
- - Respiration = Releases energy from glucose
- - Life Processes: Nutrition, Respiration, Transport...
- 1. What is an autotroph?

<!-- layout: text-light -->
<!-- purpose: quick-assessment-recap -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Assess understanding and reinforce key takeaways.
Purpose: Formative assessment.
Explain: Ask students to answer the questions on slide. Encourage peer discussion for 30 seconds before calling on individuals.
Ask: "Who can define autotroph in their own words?"
Listen for: 'Organism that makes its own food.'
Correct: Reinforce with examples like plants and algae.
Transition: Great job. We've covered the basics of nutrition and respiration.
-->
